1: <header>Environmental implications of stored cattle slurry treatment with sulphuric acid and biochar: A life cycle assessment approach </header>
Carla Miranda a, , Ana Sofia Soares Ana Cláudia Coelho b , Henrique Trindade Carlos Afonso Teixeira a

a Centre for the Research and Technology of Agro-Environmental and Biological Sciences (CITAB), Universidade de Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro, Quinta de Prados, 5000-801, Vila Real, Portugal b Animal and Veterinary Research Center (CECAV), Universidade de Trás-os-Montes e Alto Douro, Quinta de Prados, 5000-801, Vila Real, Portugal

ARTICLE INFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Animal manure GHG emissions Ammonia Acidification Life cycle assessment|With the increase of animal slurry produced from livestock production, the monitoring and mitigation of greenhouse gas (GHG) and ammonia (NH3) emissions represent a major issue. Life cycle assessment (LCA) has been used to evaluate the long-term environmental effects of applied strategies and technologies on cattle slurry management for mitigation of environmental harmful gases. This study was carried on two main aims: first, the effect of the addition of sulphuric acid (SA), biochar (SBi) or A + Bi to liquid cattle-slurry (treated systems) on gas emissions during storage compared to the untreated system (S) was investigated in a laboratory-controlled experiment; second, the environmental implications of each treated or untreated system were assessed through a LCA approach according to ISO 14040/44. Five CML 2001 impact categories were used: eutrophication potential (EP), acidification potential (AP), global warming potential (GWP), human toxicity potential (HTP) and Ozone Layer Depletion Potential (ODP). Comparisons were based on 1 ton fresh dairy cattle slurry. The envi- ronmental profile of untreated system showed lower efficiency in mitigation of total GHG and NH3 emissions (0.0312 and 0.0001 kg CO2-eq respectively), during storage period and greater impact on GWP and HTP cate- gories. The electricity consumption in mechanical separation dominated the environmental impacts. From the three proposed treated systems, SA showed the highest efficiency on mitigation of gas emissions compared to the other treatments, reducing NH3, CH4 and CO2 emissions respectively in 61%, 98% and 15%, when compared to the SBi system. In all categories, acidified slurry also showed the lowest environmental impact relative to other treated systems and the benefit was more evident when the impacts were expressed per kilogram of nitrogen in the slurry. Therefore, LCA methodology was of great importance to assess the environmental implications of the treatments and acidification can be considered as an effective technique on the mitigation of environmental implications of livestock production and cattle-effluent valorization. Optimization and uniformity of performed studies are essential to validate new strategies to improve the sustainability of this sector in the management of animal wastewater.
2: With the global growth of the livestock system and consequently increasing of the total meat production and the amount of produced slurry (a mixture of animal manure, split feed, urine and water), the concern about their environmental implications has been increasing (Steinfeld et al., 2006; De Vries et al., 2012). Therefore, meat production is expected increasing up to 455 million tons in 2050, relative to 258 million tons reported between 2005 and 2007 (Alexandratos and

Bruinsma, 2012; ten Hoeve et al., 2016). However, the slurry that is applied to agricultural land demonstrates several benefits due to its fertilizing value. Since the slurry contains nutrients essential to crop growth to allow improving the soil quality and reducing the use of mineral fertilizer and production costs. Although the management and transport of slurry require natural resources, putting pressure on the environment due to gaseous emissions that are emitted in slurry storage period and land application (Steinfeld et al., 2006; ten Hoeve et al., 2016). Areas with high livestock densities showed that the

environmental impacts are greater, where field application of cattle and pig slurry can exceed the capacity of the crops, resulting in phosphorus (P) loss to waterbodies and consequently eutrophication (Christensen et al., 2013; ten Hoeve et al., 2014; Brennan et al., 2015).

Livestock production, agriculture, land use and forestry changes are the largest contributors to anthropogenic greenhouse gas (GHG) emis- sions and hence to global warming (Leip et al., 2017; Llonch et al., 2017). Also, the changes in world climates produce in the long term effects on factors, such as average temperature, average precipitation, humidity, and average solar radiation; changes that may contribute to spread of human and animal diseases (Ahmadi et al., 2020; Hassantabar et al., 2020a, 2020b). Thus, in 2014 and update in 2018, the European Union (EU) climate and energy framework has committed to reduce GHG emissions from animal waste and agriculture by 30% in 2030, compared to 2005 (Regulation EU No. 2018/842). The two major GHG emitted by the agriculture/livestock sector are: methane (CH4), which is released mostly by enteric fermentation of ruminants, slurry storage and management and rice cultivation, and; nitrous oxide (N20), released mostly from slurry management and agricultural soils amended with synthetic or organic fertilizers. Agriculture is also the dominant responsible by atmospheric ammonia (NH3), with land application of slurry being a major component. However, in livestock building the concentrations of NH3 mainly produced by cattle is high with a severe impact on animal health and welfare, reducing the food consumption and animal growth. In addition, there are also carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions during the production and transport of animal products and feed (Leip et al., 2017; Llonch et al., 2017; Tullo et al., 2019).

In livestock systems, a variety of strategies have been developed to mitigate GHG emissions, including changes in animal breeding, feeding and slurry management practices. To reduce the impact of animal pro- duction on the environment, the aim of strategies were increasing diet digestibility and reproductive efficiency and improving productivity and animal welfare and health (Llonch et al., 2017; Laca et al., 2019). Cur- rent mitigation of slurry treatment technologies to decrease the envi- ronmental implications through the indirect mitigation of gas emissions, include improving management practices, controlled composting, reduction of animal slurry and its exploitation of energy (incineration, anaerobic digester biogas and landfill gas recovery), due to the conser- vation of fossil fuel and raw materials and improve natural resource and energy efficiency (Bogner et al., 2008).

Acidification of slurry is another strategy used to mitigate the gaseous emissions during the slurry management practices (Fangueiro et al., 2015a, 2015b; Hou et al., 2015). Effective slurry acidification can be performed with the following additives, H2SO4, HCI or HNO3 into three major management phases: animal house, storage or at field application. When the slurry is early acidified, NH3 emissions decreased in all management steps and consequently increasing N fertilizer value due to a higher of N applied into soil relative to non-acidified slurry (ten Hoeve et al., 2016). Fangueiro et al. (2015a) also reported that the slurry acidification of LF decreased significantly NH3 emissions and the global warming potential (GWP). In addition, CH4 and N2O emissions in early acidification also reduced during the storage period by the lower mi- crobial activity in the slurry (Berg et al., 2006; Ottosen et al., 2009; Sorensen and Eriksen, 2009). In the storage period, the acid addition is a process with low costs in comparison with the early acidification, although it can occur foaming (Fangueiro et al., 2015b).

As a soil amendment, biochar that resulted of pyrolyzed organic material has been demonstrated several benefits on soil fertility and at mitigating gaseous emissions (Steiner et al., 2007; Subedi et al., 2015; Geraldi et al., 2019). Biochar holds 50% of the carbon biomass and when applied into the soil it sequesters that carbon for centuries, removing the circulated CO2 and consequently reduce the amount of atmospheric CO2 (Woolf et al., 2014). Brennan et al. (2015) have also demonstrated that biochar reduces GHG emissions when slurry application.
Most of studies using the strategies previously described had as aim to mitigate gaseous emissions, although the evaluation of environmental

impacts of these treatments on slurry is unknown. In addition, it is essential to understand whether the alternatives are efficient to reduce these impacts or whether other life cycle steps and impact categories are harmed (ten Hoeve et al., 2016). Life cycle assessment (LCA) is the tool that comprises emissions and consumptions of the each life cycle phase of a system to obtain its end product or service, determining its envi- ronmental impact and to compare the analysis with other production systems (ISO 14040, 2006). Using the LCA, the environmental impact has been performed in some technologies applied to slurry, including all steps of the slurry management life cycle and its products (Lopez-Ri- daura et al., 2009; De Vries et al., 2015; ten Hoeve et al., 2016).

To increase the efficiency in mitigation of gaseous emissions, degradation of organic material and global environmental impact mainly, new strategies are need in comparison to traditional slurry management practices as the mechanical separation. From different strategies described here with these aims, two of them were chosen to assess which would be the most advantageous in reducing gaseous emissions and to the environment. Thus, the first objective of this study was to laboratory investigate the effect of liquid cattle-slurry treatments like addition of sulphuric acid and/or biochar relative to conventional practice that only include the mechanical separation on GHG and NH3 emissions during storage and the second objective was to assess the ef- ficiency of these three treatment strategies sulphuric acid, biochar and biochar + sulphuric acid, determining their environmental implications, using the LCA methodology.

In addition, it is essential to understand whether the alternatives are efficient to reduce these impacts or whether other life cycle steps and impact categories are harmed (ten Hoeve et al., 2016). Life cycle assessment (LCA) is the tool that comprises emissions and consumptions of the each life cycle phase of a system to obtain its end product or service, determining its envi- ronmental impact and to compare the analysis with other production systems (ISO 14040, 2006). Using the LCA, the environmental impact has been performed in some technologies applied to slurry, including all steps of the slurry management life cycle and its products (Lopez-Ri- daura et al., 2009; De Vries et al., 2015; ten Hoeve et al., 2016).

To increase the efficiency in mitigation of gaseous emissions, degradation of organic material and global environmental impact mainly, new strategies are need in comparison to traditional slurry management practices as the mechanical separation. From different strategies described here with these aims, two of them were chosen to assess which would be the most advantageous in reducing gaseous emissions and to the environment. Thus, the first objective of this study was to laboratory investigate the effect of liquid cattle-slurry treatments like addition of sulphuric acid and/or biochar relative to conventional practice that only include the mechanical separation on GHG and NH3 emissions during storage and the second objective was to assess the ef- ficiency of these three treatment strategies sulphuric acid, biochar and biochar + sulphuric acid, determining their environmental implications, using the LCA methodology.
7: With the global growth of the livestock system and consequently increasing of the total meat production and the amount of produced slurry (a mixture of animal manure, split feed, urine and water), the concern about their environmental implications has been increasing (Steinfeld et al., 2006; De Vries et al., 2012). Therefore, meat production is expected increasing up to 455 million tons in 2050, relative to 258 million tons reported between 2005 and 2007 (Alexandratos and

Bruinsma, 2012; ten Hoeve et al., 2016). However, the slurry that is applied to agricultural land demonstrates several benefits due to its fertilizing value. Since the slurry contains nutrients essential to crop growth to allow improving the soil quality and reducing the use of mineral fertilizer and production costs. Although the management and transport of slurry require natural resources, putting pressure on the environment due to gaseous emissions that are emitted in slurry storage period and land application (Steinfeld et al., 2006; ten Hoeve et al., 2016). Areas with high livestock densities showed that the

environmental impacts are greater, where field application of cattle and pig slurry can exceed the capacity of the crops, resulting in phosphorus (P) loss to waterbodies and consequently eutrophication (Christensen et al., 2013; ten Hoeve et al., 2014; Brennan et al., 2015).

Livestock production, agriculture, land use and forestry changes are the largest contributors to anthropogenic greenhouse gas (GHG) emis- sions and hence to global warming (Leip et al., 2017; Llonch et al., 2017). Also, the changes in world climates produce in the long term effects on factors, such as average temperature, average precipitation, humidity, and average solar radiation; changes that may contribute to spread of human and animal diseases (Ahmadi et al., 2020; Hassantabar et al., 2020a, 2020b). Thus, in 2014 and update in 2018, the European Union (EU) climate and energy framework has committed to reduce GHG emissions from animal waste and agriculture by 30% in 2030, compared to 2005 (Regulation EU No. 2018/842). The two major GHG emitted by the agriculture/livestock sector are: methane (CH4), which is released mostly by enteric fermentation of ruminants, slurry storage and management and rice cultivation, and; nitrous oxide (N20), released mostly from slurry management and agricultural soils amended with synthetic or organic fertilizers. Agriculture is also the dominant responsible by atmospheric ammonia (NH3), with land application of slurry being a major component. However, in livestock building the concentrations of NH3 mainly produced by cattle is high with a severe impact on animal health and welfare, reducing the food consumption and animal growth. In addition, there are also carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions during the production and transport of animal products and feed (Leip et al., 2017; Llonch et al., 2017; Tullo et al., 2019).

In livestock systems, a variety of strategies have been developed to mitigate GHG emissions, including changes in animal breeding, feeding and slurry management practices. To reduce the impact of animal pro- duction on the environment, the aim of strategies were increasing diet digestibility and reproductive efficiency and improving productivity and animal welfare and health (Llonch et al., 2017; Laca et al., 2019). Cur- rent mitigation of slurry treatment technologies to decrease the envi- ronmental implications through the indirect mitigation of gas emissions, include improving management practices, controlled composting, reduction of animal slurry and its exploitation of energy (incineration, anaerobic digester biogas and landfill gas recovery), due to the conser- vation of fossil fuel and raw materials and improve natural resource and energy efficiency (Bogner et al., 2008).

Acidification of slurry is another strategy used to mitigate the gaseous emissions during the slurry management practices (Fangueiro et al., 2015a, 2015b; Hou et al., 2015). Effective slurry acidification can be performed with the following additives, H2SO4, HCI or HNO3 into three major management phases: animal house, storage or at field application. When the slurry is early acidified, NH3 emissions decreased in all management steps and consequently increasing N fertilizer value due to a higher of N applied into soil relative to non-acidified slurry (ten Hoeve et al., 2016). Fangueiro et al. (2015a) also reported that the slurry acidification of LF decreased significantly NH3 emissions and the global warming potential (GWP). In addition, CH4 and N2O emissions in early acidification also reduced during the storage period by the lower mi- crobial activity in the slurry (Berg et al., 2006; Ottosen et al., 2009; Sorensen and Eriksen, 2009). In the storage period, the acid addition is a process with low costs in comparison with the early acidification, although it can occur foaming (Fangueiro et al., 2015b).

As a soil amendment, biochar that resulted of pyrolyzed organic material has been demonstrated several benefits on soil fertility and at mitigating gaseous emissions (Steiner et al., 2007; Subedi et al., 2015; Geraldi et al., 2019). Biochar holds 50% of the carbon biomass and when applied into the soil it sequesters that carbon for centuries, removing the circulated CO2 and consequently reduce the amount of atmospheric CO2 (Woolf et al., 2014). Brennan et al. (2015) have also demonstrated that biochar reduces GHG emissions when slurry application.
Most of studies using the strategies previously described had as aim to mitigate gaseous emissions, although the evaluation of environmental

impacts of these treatments on slurry is unknown. In addition, it is essential to understand whether the alternatives are efficient to reduce these impacts or whether other life cycle steps and impact categories are harmed (ten Hoeve et al., 2016). Life cycle assessment (LCA) is the tool that comprises emissions and consumptions of the each life cycle phase of a system to obtain its end product or service, determining its envi- ronmental impact and to compare the analysis with other production systems (ISO 14040, 2006). Using the LCA, the environmental impact has been performed in some technologies applied to slurry, including all steps of the slurry management life cycle and its products (Lopez-Ri- daura et al., 2009; De Vries et al., 2015; ten Hoeve et al., 2016).

To increase the efficiency in mitigation of gaseous emissions, degradation of organic material and global environmental impact mainly, new strategies are need in comparison to traditional slurry management practices as the mechanical separation. From different strategies described here with these aims, two of them were chosen to assess which would be the most advantageous in reducing gaseous emissions and to the environment. Thus, the first objective of this study was to laboratory investigate the effect of liquid cattle-slurry treatments like addition of sulphuric acid and/or biochar relative to conventional practice that only include the mechanical separation on GHG and NH3 emissions during storage and the second objective was to assess the ef- ficiency of these three treatment strategies sulphuric acid, biochar and biochar + sulphuric acid, determining their environmental implications, using the LCA methodology.

10: A hundred kg of fresh cattle slurry obtained from a dairy farm in north of Portugal was subjected to mechanical separation with a screw press separator, generating two fractions, a solid fraction (SF) and a liquid (LF). One liter of obtained LF was placed in 2L Kilner jars (Fig. 1). A total of four treatments, resulting from the application of the single products or the combination between them, were considered in this study: (I) liquid fraction acidified with addition of sulphuric acid (SA); (II) liquid fraction with addition of biochar (SBi); (III) liquid fraction with addition of sulphuric acid and biochar (SABi), and; a control (S) with LF not treated. Acidification of the liquid fraction was performed by addition of concentrated sulphuric acid (H2SO4, 96%), with continuous stirring and pH measurement, until pH 5.5 was reached, as previously described (Fangueiro et al., 2015a). Thereafter the pH values were measured weekly and readjusted when necessary. In the treated LF with biochar (SBi), 4.5% (w/v) of biochar (Piroeco Bioenergy, S.L®) obtained from wood shavings and pyrolyzed at 900 was added to the slurry. In the treatment SABi, sulphuric acid and biochar were added to the slurry to achieve the same pH and rate as in the single treatments. Four rep- licates of each treatment were considered. The jars were covered with a lid with two holes (0 = 10 mm, each) to minimize evaporation and maintained at 20 + 0.5 in a chamber with temperature control during three months.

12: <header>2.1.1. Measurement of GHG and NH3 emissions </header>

From each Kilner jar, N20, CO2 and CH4 emissions were measured by placing two butyl rubber septa and a Teflon tube to seal hermetically the jars and to allow air sampling (Fig. 1). After sealing the jars hermeti- cally, using a syringe and flushed through evacuated 20 mL gas vials the first gas sample was taken. Following the same sequential order and time interval, sampling was repeated after 10 min and 20 min of closure. Sampling of N20, CO2 and CH4 emissions were measured daily during the first 6 days, every two days up to day 30 and once a week up to day 90.

From each sample, the gas concentration was determined by gas chromatography, using a GC-2014 (Shimadzu, Japan) which comprised of three detectors: an electron capture 63Ni detector, a flame ionization

Fig. 1. Scheme of the laboratory assay performed in this study, including the four considered treatments: liquid fraction with the addition of sulphuric acid (SA), biochar (SBi), sulphuric acid plus biochar (SABi) and untreated liquid fraction (S). The gaseous samples collected during three months were analyzed by gas chromatography (CG) and automated segmented-flow spectrophotometry (ASFS).

detector and a thermal conductivity detector for N2O, CH4 and CO2 analysis, respectively. The NH3 emissions were measured by the acid traps method at the same intervals as the previously gases of incubation. An acid trap with 50 mL of 0.02M orthophosphoric acid was hung above the slurry inside the Kilner jars during 1 h. Each collected acid solution was preserved at 4 °C and posteriorly analyzed by automated segmented-flow spectrophotometry, for total ammonium N content (Houba et al., 1995; Fangueiro et al., 2015a).
From each system, the cumulative gas emissions were calculated, considering the averaging the GHG and NH3 flux rates from cattle-slurry storage and the time interval between sampling.

14: The used framework and methodology followed the ISO 14040 and ISO 14044 standards. According to LCA principles standards, this study was organized in four main sections: goal and scope definition including the functional unit, analyzed systems and system boundary, life cycle data inventory and assumptions, environmental impact assessment and its result interpretation (ISO 14040, 2006; ISO 14044, 2006).
15: <header>2.2.1. Goal and scope of the LCA </header>

The aim of this study was to characterize the conventional practice of slurry management (mechanical separation, without added treatment) and assess the slurry treatment efficiency and the environmental impact potential of the addition of sulphuric acid and biochar individually on liquid slurry and whether there was synergetic environmental benefit from combining both technologies, after slurry mechanical separation. Moreover, the gas emissions obtained in the experiment under labora- tory conditions previously described, were used and adapted for a farm scale. In the different studied production systems, the environmental impact assessment was performed by LCA method (ISO 14040, 2006).
For treatments comparison, the functional unit (FU) on this study was the management of 1 ton of excreted fresh whole slurry (for a time unit of one day) by lactating cows under standard conditions.

2.2.1.1. Systems. For processing dairy cattle slurry, three treatment systems were considered and assessed the environmental impact relative to a reference system, in which slurry liquid fraction was not treated (S):

<list>I. Liquid fraction with addition of sulphuric acid (SA) 
II. Liquid fraction with addition of biochar (SBi) 
III. Liquid fraction with addition of sulphuric and acid biochar (SABi)</list>

In all systems (treated and untreated) the mechanical separation of dairy cattle slurry was performed, resulting a liquid and solid fraction from a screw press separator. The used slurry was fresh, collected in the same day and all slurry was assumed to be stored in a covered outside tank for a period of three months. Additionally, unit processes in liquid slurry was the addition of sulphuric acid for the SA system, biochar for the SBi system and the addition of both additives for the SABi system (Fig. 2). In the assessment, consumptions and resource use, and all emissions for the process were included. For the SF in the composting process was assumed that was covered with an air-tight lining in long rows (windrows) well-aerated, turning every second week. After the storage and composting, the end products of both fractions were then applied to the land as fertilizers.

2.2.1.2. System boundary. The environmental impacts were analyzed in four slurry processing systems, dividing in the main unit processes, such as the liquid-solid separation of slurry production, treatment fractions and the storage period, and the transportation of the end products for field application. The system also included the production of sulphuric acid and biochar used for cattle-slurry treatment, the consumed

Fig. 2. System boundary of the four slurry processing systems analyzed in this study, considering a functional unit (FU) of 1 ton of fresh slurry. System S (not treated), SA (sulphuric acid), SBi (biochar), SABi (sulphuric acid and biochar). LF: liquid fraction. E: electricity consumption. T: transportation.

electricity on mechanical separation and storage, and fuel in transport of slurry and products for slurry treatment (Fig. 2). The transport of slurry fractions is performed in heavy-duty vehicles like lorry (20-28 tones of load capacity) using diesel, assuming an average distance of 5 km be- tween the farm and storage site and 10 km until the field application. The processes excluded the animal production and its impacts from the equipment used and physical buildings, since the slurry management would not be influenced by a modification in the animal production sector. Although the capital goods production (buildings and machin- ery) is not considered, the gaseous emissions resulted from the energy inputs (electricity and diesel) and production of material are considered in the analyzed systems.
2.2.2. Life cycle data inventory and assumptions

The inventory of the lifecycle (LCI) of each steps of slurry manage- ment was based on the process approach to capture the energy, emis- sions and material flows (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014), considering system parameters and local conditions through the GaBi 6 software (Table 1). The LCI requires data from various sources, such as verifiable databases and representative literature reviews, and additionally, a field study has been conducted allowing the data characterization.

All systems comprised three basic slurry unit-processes like me- chanical separation, storage and field application of liquid fraction, in which in the treated systems (SA, SBi and SABi) was added additives (sulphuric acid, biochar and both, respectively) during storage period (Table
2.2.2.1. Mechanical separation. The management of slurry will affect its physical and chemical characteristics. The slurry produced in-house was
Assumptions and resource used for 1 ton of slurry between farm and field application.

Unit process|Systems|Assumptions Mechanical separation|All*|Screw press Electricity (0.5 kWh ton slurry) Storage and Treatment|All|Transport to storage (5 km) Agitation before unloading (0.25 kWh ton slurry) ||Electricity to slurry mixer (0.5 kWh ton slurry) Three months storage |SA|Addition of sulphuric acid 96% (to pH 5.5) |SBi|Addition of biochar (4.5%) |SABi|Addition of sulphuric acid 96% (to pH 5.5) and biochar (4.5%) Field application|All|Application of solids and liquid as organic fertilizer ||Liquid fractions are applied to closer areas (10 km)
*All: S (not treated), SA (sulphuric acid), SBi (biochar), SABi (sulphuric acid and biochar) of liquid fraction.

collected and solid-liquid separation unit process was conducted to divide slurry into two fractions. This process resulted in a SF and a LF in the proportions of 20% and 80% by mass, respectively, using a screw press separator (Fangueiro et al., 2008a). At the beginning of the storage phase both whole slurry and slurry fractions were characterized and can be found in Table 2 (Soares et al., 2019). The electricity consumption (Table 1 and S1) during the screw press separation was 0.5 kWh per 1 ton of slurry (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014). The efficiency of the
Table 2 Excreted slurry and fractions characteristics in this study (Soares et al., 2019).

Systems|Effluent or fraction|DM (%)|pH|EC (mS cm|NH4 (mg kg|NO3|Total N (g kg 1)|Organic C|P|K Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation All*|Slurry|8.5|8.5|4.7|213.1|<0.1|2.7|397.5|5.3|34.2 All|LF|4.6|8.5|3.5|159.0|<0.1|1.8|351.2|8.4|59.1 All|SF|13.7|8.5|2.1|46.8|<0.1|1.8|392.2|3.7|18.5 After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage S|LF|5.1|8.8|3.1|45.2|<0.1|0.65|300.3|9.0|60.4 SA|LF|6.8|6.5|4.1|154.0|<0.1|2.08|246.6|6.5|52.6 SBi|LF|8.7|8.9|2.7|11.6|<0.1|0.97|529.8|4.8|45.5 SABi|LF|9.3|6.9|4.9|78.1|<0.1|1.58|493.9|3.6|33.2
*All included the system S (not treated), SA (sulphuric acid), SBi (biochar), SABi (sulphuric acid and biochar). LF: liquid fraction. SF: solid fraction.

separation process of the solid and liquid components of slurry (Tables 1 and 2) was estimated using the percentage of removal varied 15-69% for P and between 10 and 33% for N (Fangueiro et al., 2008a; Hjorth et al., 2010; Riaño and García-González et al., 2014; ten Hoeve et al., 2014). The SF was composted before field application; electricity, trans- portation and emissions from this fraction were not included.

2.2.2.2. Storage and treatment with sulphuric acid and biochar. In all systems, both fractions were stored close to the farm for a period of three months, over an average distance of 5 km from animal facilities. In addition, the acidification of LF included the weekly acid addition (sulphuric acid, 96% H2SO4) in outside storage acidification systems (SA and SABi) and to reach pH 5.5, with on average 9.7 kg per ton of slurry (Sorensen and Eriksen, 2009). The addition of biochar to liquid slurry at the beginning of storage in SBi and SABi treatments was performed with, on average, 45 kg per ton slurry. Slurry fractions were also characterized at the end of the storage period (Table 2).
For all systems, N2O, CO2, CH4 and NH3 emissions during storage and treatment were derived from the results obtained at the laboratory experiment previously described.

2.2.2.3. Field application. In all systems, LF and SF are applied to crop land in substitution of chemical fertilizers. The area required for the application of 1 ton dairy slurry was calculated as 377 m² for the reference system. The solid fractions were transported from the stored cattle farm and applied at a maximum of 30 kg P ha as this is assumed to be the maximum annual P demand of commonly grown crops (ten Hoeve et al., 2016). Although GHG emissions and NH3 differ with studied treatments during the field application was not measured spe- cifically for this study, referenced data have been used for the gas emissions considering the same results for all pathways (Table 3).
Life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) accounted and categorized the contribution of the resource and consumptions uses, and all emissions

from the systems and references to each analyzed environmental impact category (De Vries et al., 2012). The environmental profile was obtained using the GaBi 6 software and CML 2001 impact assessment method. Five environmental impact categories used in other LCA studies (De Vries et al., 2012; Salvador et al., 2016; Manso et al., 2018) were assessed to their importance for slurry management and quantified using life cycle assessment: eutrophication potential (EP, in which the NH3, NO emissions, and leaching of NO3, were expressed in kg PO acidification potential (AP, the NH3, NOx, and SO2 emissions were expressed in kg SO2-eq), global warming potential (GWP, including CO2, CH4, and N2O emissions that were expressed in kg CO2-eq), human toxicity potential (HTP) and Ozone Layer Depletion Potential (ODP). In GWP, a period of 100 years was considered for the emission effects from the moment they reached the atmosphere (1 kg N2O = 265 kg CO2-eq and 1 kg CH4 = 28 kg CO2-eq) (Myhre, 2013). The five impact categories were expressed either per functional unit (1 ton of slurry) or per slurry nitrogen value (1 kg of slurry N).

The solidity of the results was obtained by the sensitivity analysis based on the comparison between the references and systems influence of underlying assumptions and changes in relevant parameters (De Vries et al., 2012).
16: <header>2.2.1. Goal and scope of the LCA </header>

The aim of this study was to characterize the conventional practice of slurry management (mechanical separation, without added treatment) and assess the slurry treatment efficiency and the environmental impact potential of the addition of sulphuric acid and biochar individually on liquid slurry and whether there was synergetic environmental benefit from combining both technologies, after slurry mechanical separation. Moreover, the gas emissions obtained in the experiment under labora- tory conditions previously described, were used and adapted for a farm scale. In the different studied production systems, the environmental impact assessment was performed by LCA method (ISO 14040, 2006).
For treatments comparison, the functional unit (FU) on this study was the management of 1 ton of excreted fresh whole slurry (for a time unit of one day) by lactating cows under standard conditions.

2.2.1.1. Systems. For processing dairy cattle slurry, three treatment systems were considered and assessed the environmental impact relative to a reference system, in which slurry liquid fraction was not treated (S):

<list>I. Liquid fraction with addition of sulphuric acid (SA) 
II. Liquid fraction with addition of biochar (SBi) 
III. Liquid fraction with addition of sulphuric and acid biochar (SABi)</list>

In all systems (treated and untreated) the mechanical separation of dairy cattle slurry was performed, resulting a liquid and solid fraction from a screw press separator. The used slurry was fresh, collected in the same day and all slurry was assumed to be stored in a covered outside tank for a period of three months. Additionally, unit processes in liquid slurry was the addition of sulphuric acid for the SA system, biochar for the SBi system and the addition of both additives for the SABi system (Fig. 2). In the assessment, consumptions and resource use, and all emissions for the process were included. For the SF in the composting process was assumed that was covered with an air-tight lining in long rows (windrows) well-aerated, turning every second week. After the storage and composting, the end products of both fractions were then applied to the land as fertilizers.

2.2.1.2. System boundary. The environmental impacts were analyzed in four slurry processing systems, dividing in the main unit processes, such as the liquid-solid separation of slurry production, treatment fractions and the storage period, and the transportation of the end products for field application. The system also included the production of sulphuric acid and biochar used for cattle-slurry treatment, the consumed

Fig. 2. System boundary of the four slurry processing systems analyzed in this study, considering a functional unit (FU) of 1 ton of fresh slurry. System S (not treated), SA (sulphuric acid), SBi (biochar), SABi (sulphuric acid and biochar). LF: liquid fraction. E: electricity consumption. T: transportation.

electricity on mechanical separation and storage, and fuel in transport of slurry and products for slurry treatment (Fig. 2). The transport of slurry fractions is performed in heavy-duty vehicles like lorry (20-28 tones of load capacity) using diesel, assuming an average distance of 5 km be- tween the farm and storage site and 10 km until the field application. The processes excluded the animal production and its impacts from the equipment used and physical buildings, since the slurry management would not be influenced by a modification in the animal production sector. Although the capital goods production (buildings and machin- ery) is not considered, the gaseous emissions resulted from the energy inputs (electricity and diesel) and production of material are considered in the analyzed systems.
2.2.2. Life cycle data inventory and assumptions

The inventory of the lifecycle (LCI) of each steps of slurry manage- ment was based on the process approach to capture the energy, emis- sions and material flows (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014), considering system parameters and local conditions through the GaBi 6 software (Table 1). The LCI requires data from various sources, such as verifiable databases and representative literature reviews, and additionally, a field study has been conducted allowing the data characterization.

All systems comprised three basic slurry unit-processes like me- chanical separation, storage and field application of liquid fraction, in which in the treated systems (SA, SBi and SABi) was added additives (sulphuric acid, biochar and both, respectively) during storage period (Table

This process resulted in a SF and a LF in the proportions of 20% and 80% by mass, respectively, using a screw press separator (Fangueiro et al., 2008a). At the beginning of the storage phase both whole slurry and slurry fractions were characterized and can be found in Table 2 (Soares et al., 2019). The electricity consumption (Table 1 and S1) during the screw press separation was 0.5 kWh per 1 ton of slurry (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014). The efficiency of the
Table 2 Excreted slurry and fractions characteristics in this study (Soares et al., 2019).

Systems|Effluent or fraction|DM (%)|pH|EC (mS cm|NH4 (mg kg|NO3|Total N (g kg 1)|Organic C|P|K Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation All*|Slurry|8.5|8.5|4.7|213.1|<0.1|2.7|397.5|5.3|34.2 All|LF|4.6|8.5|3.5|159.0|<0.1|1.8|351.2|8.4|59.1 All|SF|13.7|8.5|2.1|46.8|<0.1|1.8|392.2|3.7|18.5 After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage S|LF|5.1|8.8|3.1|45.2|<0.1|0.65|300.3|9.0|60.4 SA|LF|6.8|6.5|4.1|154.0|<0.1|2.08|246.6|6.5|52.6 SBi|LF|8.7|8.9|2.7|11.6|<0.1|0.97|529.8|4.8|45.5 SABi|LF|9.3|6.9|4.9|78.1|<0.1|1.58|493.9|3.6|33.2
*All included the system S (not treated), SA (sulphuric acid), SBi (biochar), SABi (sulphuric acid and biochar). LF: liquid fraction. SF: solid fraction.

separation process of the solid and liquid components of slurry (Tables 1 and 2) was estimated using the percentage of removal varied 15-69% for P and between 10 and 33% for N (Fangueiro et al., 2008a; Hjorth et al., 2010; Riaño and García-González et al., 2014; ten Hoeve et al., 2014). The SF was composted before field application; electricity, trans- portation and emissions from this fraction were not included.

2.2.2.2. Storage and treatment with sulphuric acid and biochar. In all systems, both fractions were stored close to the farm for a period of three months, over an average distance of 5 km from animal facilities. In addition, the acidification of LF included the weekly acid addition (sulphuric acid, 96% H2SO4) in outside storage acidification systems (SA and SABi) and to reach pH 5.5, with on average 9.7 kg per ton of slurry (Sorensen and Eriksen, 2009). The addition of biochar to liquid slurry at the beginning of storage in SBi and SABi treatments was performed with, on average, 45 kg per ton slurry. Slurry fractions were also characterized at the end of the storage period (Table 2).
For all systems, N2O, CO2, CH4 and NH3 emissions during storage and treatment were derived from the results obtained at the laboratory experiment previously described.

2.2.2.3. Field application. In all systems, LF and SF are applied to crop land in substitution of chemical fertilizers. The area required for the application of 1 ton dairy slurry was calculated as 377 m² for the reference system. The solid fractions were transported from the stored cattle farm and applied at a maximum of 30 kg P ha as this is assumed to be the maximum annual P demand of commonly grown crops (ten Hoeve et al., 2016). Although GHG emissions and NH3 differ with studied treatments during the field application was not measured spe- cifically for this study, referenced data have been used for the gas emissions considering the same results for all pathways (Table 3).
Life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) accounted and categorized the contribution of the resource and consumptions uses, and all emissions

from the systems and references to each analyzed environmental impact category (De Vries et al., 2012). The environmental profile was obtained using the GaBi 6 software and CML 2001 impact assessment method. Five environmental impact categories used in other LCA studies (De Vries et al., 2012; Salvador et al., 2016; Manso et al., 2018) were assessed to their importance for slurry management and quantified using life cycle assessment: eutrophication potential (EP, in which the NH3, NO emissions, and leaching of NO3, were expressed in kg PO acidification potential (AP, the NH3, NOx, and SO2 emissions were expressed in kg SO2-eq), global warming potential (GWP, including CO2, CH4, and N2O emissions that were expressed in kg CO2-eq), human toxicity potential (HTP) and Ozone Layer Depletion Potential (ODP). In GWP, a period of 100 years was considered for the emission effects from the moment they reached the atmosphere (1 kg N2O = 265 kg CO2-eq and 1 kg CH4 = 28 kg CO2-eq) (Myhre, 2013). The five impact categories were expressed either per functional unit (1 ton of slurry) or per slurry nitrogen value (1 kg of slurry N).

The solidity of the results was obtained by the sensitivity analysis based on the comparison between the references and systems influence of underlying assumptions and changes in relevant parameters (De Vries et al., 2012).
19: <header>2.2.1. Goal and scope of the LCA </header>

The aim of this study was to characterize the conventional practice of slurry management (mechanical separation, without added treatment) and assess the slurry treatment efficiency and the environmental impact potential of the addition of sulphuric acid and biochar individually on liquid slurry and whether there was synergetic environmental benefit from combining both technologies, after slurry mechanical separation. Moreover, the gas emissions obtained in the experiment under labora- tory conditions previously described, were used and adapted for a farm scale. In the different studied production systems, the environmental impact assessment was performed by LCA method (ISO 14040, 2006).
For treatments comparison, the functional unit (FU) on this study was the management of 1 ton of excreted fresh whole slurry (for a time unit of one day) by lactating cows under standard conditions.

2.2.1.1. Systems. For processing dairy cattle slurry, three treatment systems were considered and assessed the environmental impact relative to a reference system, in which slurry liquid fraction was not treated (S):

<list>I. Liquid fraction with addition of sulphuric acid (SA) 
II. Liquid fraction with addition of biochar (SBi) 
III. Liquid fraction with addition of sulphuric and acid biochar (SABi)</list>

In all systems (treated and untreated) the mechanical separation of dairy cattle slurry was performed, resulting a liquid and solid fraction from a screw press separator. The used slurry was fresh, collected in the same day and all slurry was assumed to be stored in a covered outside tank for a period of three months. Additionally, unit processes in liquid slurry was the addition of sulphuric acid for the SA system, biochar for the SBi system and the addition of both additives for the SABi system (Fig. 2). In the assessment, consumptions and resource use, and all emissions for the process were included. For the SF in the composting process was assumed that was covered with an air-tight lining in long rows (windrows) well-aerated, turning every second week. After the storage and composting, the end products of both fractions were then applied to the land as fertilizers.

2.2.1.2. System boundary. The environmental impacts were analyzed in four slurry processing systems, dividing in the main unit processes, such as the liquid-solid separation of slurry production, treatment fractions and the storage period, and the transportation of the end products for field application. The system also included the production of sulphuric acid and biochar used for cattle-slurry treatment, the consumed

Fig. 2. System boundary of the four slurry processing systems analyzed in this study, considering a functional unit (FU) of 1 ton of fresh slurry. System S (not treated), SA (sulphuric acid), SBi (biochar), SABi (sulphuric acid and biochar). LF: liquid fraction. E: electricity consumption. T: transportation.

electricity on mechanical separation and storage, and fuel in transport of slurry and products for slurry treatment (Fig. 2). The transport of slurry fractions is performed in heavy-duty vehicles like lorry (20-28 tones of load capacity) using diesel, assuming an average distance of 5 km be- tween the farm and storage site and 10 km until the field application. The processes excluded the animal production and its impacts from the equipment used and physical buildings, since the slurry management would not be influenced by a modification in the animal production sector. Although the capital goods production (buildings and machin- ery) is not considered, the gaseous emissions resulted from the energy inputs (electricity and diesel) and production of material are considered in the analyzed systems.
2.2.2. Life cycle data inventory and assumptions

The inventory of the lifecycle (LCI) of each steps of slurry manage- ment was based on the process approach to capture the energy, emis- sions and material flows (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014), considering system parameters and local conditions through the GaBi 6 software (Table 1). The LCI requires data from various sources, such as verifiable databases and representative literature reviews, and additionally, a field study has been conducted allowing the data characterization.

All systems comprised three basic slurry unit-processes like me- chanical separation, storage and field application of liquid fraction, in which in the treated systems (SA, SBi and SABi) was added additives (sulphuric acid, biochar and both, respectively) during storage period (Table

collected and solid-liquid separation unit process was conducted to divide slurry into two fractions. This process resulted in a SF and a LF in the proportions of 20% and 80% by mass, respectively, using a screw press separator (Fangueiro et al., 2008a). At the beginning of the storage phase both whole slurry and slurry fractions were characterized and can be found in Table 2 (Soares et al., 2019). The electricity consumption (Table 1 and S1) during the screw press separation was 0.5 kWh per 1 ton of slurry (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014). The efficiency of the
Table 2 Excreted slurry and fractions characteristics in this study (Soares et al., 2019).

Systems|Effluent or fraction|DM (%)|pH|EC (mS cm|NH4 (mg kg|NO3|Total N (g kg 1)|Organic C|P|K Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation|Mechanical separation All*|Slurry|8.5|8.5|4.7|213.1|<0.1|2.7|397.5|5.3|34.2 All|LF|4.6|8.5|3.5|159.0|<0.1|1.8|351.2|8.4|59.1 All|SF|13.7|8.5|2.1|46.8|<0.1|1.8|392.2|3.7|18.5 After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage|After 90 days of storage S|LF|5.1|8.8|3.1|45.2|<0.1|0.65|300.3|9.0|60.4 SA|LF|6.8|6.5|4.1|154.0|<0.1|2.08|246.6|6.5|52.6 SBi|LF|8.7|8.9|2.7|11.6|<0.1|0.97|529.8|4.8|45.5 SABi|LF|9.3|6.9|4.9|78.1|<0.1|1.58|493.9|3.6|33.2
*All included the system S (not treated), SA (sulphuric acid), SBi (biochar), SABi (sulphuric acid and biochar). LF: liquid fraction. SF: solid fraction.

separation process of the solid and liquid components of slurry (Tables 1 and 2) was estimated using the percentage of removal varied 15-69% for P and between 10 and 33% for N (Fangueiro et al., 2008a; Hjorth et al., 2010; Riaño and García-González et al., 2014; ten Hoeve et al., 2014). The SF was composted before field application; electricity, trans- portation and emissions from this fraction were not included.

2.2.2.2. Storage and treatment with sulphuric acid and biochar. In all systems, both fractions were stored close to the farm for a period of three months, over an average distance of 5 km from animal facilities. In addition, the acidification of LF included the weekly acid addition (sulphuric acid, 96% H2SO4) in outside storage acidification systems (SA and SABi) and to reach pH 5.5, with on average 9.7 kg per ton of slurry (Sorensen and Eriksen, 2009). The addition of biochar to liquid slurry at the beginning of storage in SBi and SABi treatments was performed with, on average, 45 kg per ton slurry. Slurry fractions were also characterized at the end of the storage period (Table 2).
For all systems, N2O, CO2, CH4 and NH3 emissions during storage and treatment were derived from the results obtained at the laboratory experiment previously described.

2.2.2.3. Field application. In all systems, LF and SF are applied to crop land in substitution of chemical fertilizers. The area required for the application of 1 ton dairy slurry was calculated as 377 m² for the reference system. The solid fractions were transported from the stored cattle farm and applied at a maximum of 30 kg P ha as this is assumed to be the maximum annual P demand of commonly grown crops (ten Hoeve et al., 2016). Although GHG emissions and NH3 differ with studied treatments during the field application was not measured spe- cifically for this study, referenced data have been used for the gas emissions considering the same results for all pathways (Table 3).
Life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) accounted and categorized the contribution of the resource and consumptions uses, and all emissions

from the systems and references to each analyzed environmental impact category (De Vries et al., 2012). The environmental profile was obtained using the GaBi 6 software and CML 2001 impact assessment method. Five environmental impact categories used in other LCA studies (De Vries et al., 2012; Salvador et al., 2016; Manso et al., 2018) were assessed to their importance for slurry management and quantified using life cycle assessment: eutrophication potential (EP, in which the NH3, NO emissions, and leaching of NO3, were expressed in kg PO acidification potential (AP, the NH3, NOx, and SO2 emissions were expressed in kg SO2-eq), global warming potential (GWP, including CO2, CH4, and N2O emissions that were expressed in kg CO2-eq), human toxicity potential (HTP) and Ozone Layer Depletion Potential (ODP). In GWP, a period of 100 years was considered for the emission effects from the moment they reached the atmosphere (1 kg N2O = 265 kg CO2-eq and 1 kg CH4 = 28 kg CO2-eq) (Myhre, 2013). The five impact categories were expressed either per functional unit (1 ton of slurry) or per slurry nitrogen value (1 kg of slurry N).

The solidity of the results was obtained by the sensitivity analysis based on the comparison between the references and systems influence of underlying assumptions and changes in relevant parameters (De Vries et al., 2012).
22: <header>2.2.1. Goal and scope of the LCA </header>

The aim of this study was to characterize the conventional practice of slurry management (mechanical separation, without added treatment) and assess the slurry treatment efficiency and the environmental impact potential of the addition of sulphuric acid and biochar individually on liquid slurry and whether there was synergetic environmental benefit from combining both technologies, after slurry mechanical separation. Moreover, the gas emissions obtained in the experiment under labora- tory conditions previously described, were used and adapted for a farm scale. In the different studied production systems, the environmental impact assessment was performed by LCA method (ISO 14040, 2006).
For treatments comparison, the functional unit (FU) on this study was the management of 1 ton of excreted fresh whole slurry (for a time unit of one day) by lactating cows under standard conditions.

2.2.1.1. Systems. For processing dairy cattle slurry, three treatment systems were considered and assessed the environmental impact relative to a reference system, in which slurry liquid fraction was not treated (S):

<list>I. Liquid fraction with addition of sulphuric acid (SA) 
II. Liquid fraction with addition of biochar (SBi) 
III. Liquid fraction with addition of sulphuric and acid biochar (SABi)</list>

In all systems (treated and untreated) the mechanical separation of dairy cattle slurry was performed, resulting a liquid and solid fraction from a screw press separator. The used slurry was fresh, collected in the same day and all slurry was assumed to be stored in a covered outside tank for a period of three months. Additionally, unit processes in liquid slurry was the addition of sulphuric acid for the SA system, biochar for the SBi system and the addition of both additives for the SABi system (Fig. 2). In the assessment, consumptions and resource use, and all emissions for the process were included. For the SF in the composting process was assumed that was covered with an air-tight lining in long rows (windrows) well-aerated, turning every second week. After the storage and composting, the end products of both fractions were then applied to the land as fertilizers.

2.2.1.2. System boundary. The environmental impacts were analyzed in four slurry processing systems, dividing in the main unit processes, such as the liquid-solid separation of slurry production, treatment fractions and the storage period, and the transportation of the end products for field application. The system also included the production of sulphuric acid and biochar used for cattle-slurry treatment, the consumed

Fig. 2. System boundary of the four slurry processing systems analyzed in this study, considering a functional unit (FU) of 1 ton of fresh slurry. System S (not treated), SA (sulphuric acid), SBi (biochar), SABi (sulphuric acid and biochar). LF: liquid fraction. E: electricity consumption. T: transportation.

electricity on mechanical separation and storage, and fuel in transport of slurry and products for slurry treatment (Fig. 2). The transport of slurry fractions is performed in heavy-duty vehicles like lorry (20-28 tones of load capacity) using diesel, assuming an average distance of 5 km be- tween the farm and storage site and 10 km until the field application. The processes excluded the animal production and its impacts from the equipment used and physical buildings, since the slurry management would not be influenced by a modification in the animal production sector. Although the capital goods production (buildings and machin- ery) is not considered, the gaseous emissions resulted from the energy inputs (electricity and diesel) and production of material are considered in the analyzed systems.
2.2.2. Life cycle data inventory and assumptions

The inventory of the lifecycle (LCI) of each steps of slurry manage- ment was based on the process approach to capture the energy, emis- sions and material flows (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014), considering system parameters and local conditions through the GaBi 6 software (Table 1). The LCI requires data from various sources, such as verifiable databases and representative literature reviews, and additionally, a field study has been conducted allowing the data characterization.

separation process of the solid and liquid components of slurry (Tables 1 and 2) was estimated using the percentage of removal varied 15-69% for P and between 10 and 33% for N (Fangueiro et al., 2008a; Hjorth et al., 2010; Riaño and García-González et al., 2014; ten Hoeve et al., 2014). The SF was composted before field application; electricity, trans- portation and emissions from this fraction were not included.

2.2.2.2. Storage and treatment with sulphuric acid and biochar. In all systems, both fractions were stored close to the farm for a period of three months, over an average distance of 5 km from animal facilities. In addition, the acidification of LF included the weekly acid addition (sulphuric acid, 96% H2SO4) in outside storage acidification systems (SA and SABi) and to reach pH 5.5, with on average 9.7 kg per ton of slurry (Sorensen and Eriksen, 2009). The addition of biochar to liquid slurry at the beginning of storage in SBi and SABi treatments was performed with, on average, 45 kg per ton slurry. Slurry fractions were also characterized at the end of the storage period (Table 2).
For all systems, N2O, CO2, CH4 and NH3 emissions during storage and treatment were derived from the results obtained at the laboratory experiment previously described.

2.2.2.3. Field application. In all systems, LF and SF are applied to crop land in substitution of chemical fertilizers. The area required for the application of 1 ton dairy slurry was calculated as 377 m² for the reference system. The solid fractions were transported from the stored cattle farm and applied at a maximum of 30 kg P ha as this is assumed to be the maximum annual P demand of commonly grown crops (ten Hoeve et al., 2016). Although GHG emissions and NH3 differ with studied treatments during the field application was not measured spe- cifically for this study, referenced data have been used for the gas emissions considering the same results for all pathways (Table 3).
Life cycle impact assessment (LCIA) accounted and categorized the contribution of the resource and consumptions uses, and all emissions

from the systems and references to each analyzed environmental impact category (De Vries et al., 2012). The environmental profile was obtained using the GaBi 6 software and CML 2001 impact assessment method. Five environmental impact categories used in other LCA studies (De Vries et al., 2012; Salvador et al., 2016; Manso et al., 2018) were assessed to their importance for slurry management and quantified using life cycle assessment: eutrophication potential (EP, in which the NH3, NO emissions, and leaching of NO3, were expressed in kg PO acidification potential (AP, the NH3, NOx, and SO2 emissions were expressed in kg SO2-eq), global warming potential (GWP, including CO2, CH4, and N2O emissions that were expressed in kg CO2-eq), human toxicity potential (HTP) and Ozone Layer Depletion Potential (ODP). In GWP, a period of 100 years was considered for the emission effects from the moment they reached the atmosphere (1 kg N2O = 265 kg CO2-eq and 1 kg CH4 = 28 kg CO2-eq) (Myhre, 2013). The five impact categories were expressed either per functional unit (1 ton of slurry) or per slurry nitrogen value (1 kg of slurry N).

The solidity of the results was obtained by the sensitivity analysis based on the comparison between the references and systems influence of underlying assumptions and changes in relevant parameters (De Vries et al., 2012).
25: <header>2.2.1. Goal and scope of the LCA </header>

The aim of this study was to characterize the conventional practice of slurry management (mechanical separation, without added treatment) and assess the slurry treatment efficiency and the environmental impact potential of the addition of sulphuric acid and biochar individually on liquid slurry and whether there was synergetic environmental benefit from combining both technologies, after slurry mechanical separation. Moreover, the gas emissions obtained in the experiment under labora- tory conditions previously described, were used and adapted for a farm scale. In the different studied production systems, the environmental impact assessment was performed by LCA method (ISO 14040, 2006).
For treatments comparison, the functional unit (FU) on this study was the management of 1 ton of excreted fresh whole slurry (for a time unit of one day) by lactating cows under standard conditions.

2.2.1.1. Systems. For processing dairy cattle slurry, three treatment systems were considered and assessed the environmental impact relative to a reference system, in which slurry liquid fraction was not treated (S):

<list>I. Liquid fraction with addition of sulphuric acid (SA) 
II. Liquid fraction with addition of biochar (SBi) 
III. Liquid fraction with addition of sulphuric and acid biochar (SABi)</list>

In all systems (treated and untreated) the mechanical separation of dairy cattle slurry was performed, resulting a liquid and solid fraction from a screw press separator. The used slurry was fresh, collected in the same day and all slurry was assumed to be stored in a covered outside tank for a period of three months. Additionally, unit processes in liquid slurry was the addition of sulphuric acid for the SA system, biochar for the SBi system and the addition of both additives for the SABi system (Fig. 2). In the assessment, consumptions and resource use, and all emissions for the process were included. For the SF in the composting process was assumed that was covered with an air-tight lining in long rows (windrows) well-aerated, turning every second week. After the storage and composting, the end products of both fractions were then applied to the land as fertilizers.

2.2.1.2. System boundary. The environmental impacts were analyzed in four slurry processing systems, dividing in the main unit processes, such as the liquid-solid separation of slurry production, treatment fractions and the storage period, and the transportation of the end products for field application. The system also included the production of sulphuric acid and biochar used for cattle-slurry treatment, the consumed

Fig. 2. System boundary of the four slurry processing systems analyzed in this study, considering a functional unit (FU) of 1 ton of fresh slurry. System S (not treated), SA (sulphuric acid), SBi (biochar), SABi (sulphuric acid and biochar). LF: liquid fraction. E: electricity consumption. T: transportation.

electricity on mechanical separation and storage, and fuel in transport of slurry and products for slurry treatment (Fig. 2). The transport of slurry fractions is performed in heavy-duty vehicles like lorry (20-28 tones of load capacity) using diesel, assuming an average distance of 5 km be- tween the farm and storage site and 10 km until the field application. The processes excluded the animal production and its impacts from the equipment used and physical buildings, since the slurry management would not be influenced by a modification in the animal production sector. Although the capital goods production (buildings and machin- ery) is not considered, the gaseous emissions resulted from the energy inputs (electricity and diesel) and production of material are considered in the analyzed systems.
2.2.2. Life cycle data inventory and assumptions

The inventory of the lifecycle (LCI) of each steps of slurry manage- ment was based on the process approach to capture the energy, emis- sions and material flows (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014), considering system parameters and local conditions through the GaBi 6 software (Table 1). The LCI requires data from various sources, such as verifiable databases and representative literature reviews, and additionally, a field study has been conducted allowing the data characterization.

29: <header>3.2. Treated systems with sulphuric acid and/or biochar </header>

The LCIA results for the selected categories showed a positive in- fluence of the acidificated system (system SA) compared to other treated systems. The system SABi followed by SBi were the systems with the highest environmental impact in all categories, AP, HTP and GWP mainly (Fig. 3 and Table 4). However, the occurred emissions from storage period have shown the largest reduction in the system SA, fol- lowed by SABi for the NH3, CH4, N2O and CO2 (Table 3) relative to SBi system. The total GHG emissions in analyzed treated systems, SA, SBi and SABi are 0.003, 0.063 and 0.007 kg CO2-eq of slurry during storage period, respectively. In addition, the NH3 emissions were also lower in the system SA and SABi than the system SBi. Thus, compared to the SBi system, the acidification process allowed reducing NH3, CH4 and CO2 emissions in 61%, 98% and 15% respectively, and the SABi demon- strated a reduction of 34% to NH3, 91% to CH4 and 14% to CO2 emis- sions. However, SABi system showed lower efficiency on reduction of N2O emissions than SA and SBi systems. The SBi showed greenhouse gas emissions higher than other treatments, except in N2O emissions with equal value relative to SA.

The environmental profile of the treated systems was analyzed from three unit process of slurry management like mechanical separation, storage with addition of additives and field application (Fig. 2). The environmental impact was dominated by storage phase. However, the mechanical separation and field application had little influence on the environmental profile, showing that resources use associated with slurry separation and field application (electricity and transportation in oper- ation of tractor and slurry spreader mainly) was very similar in all sys- items. The contribution of energy use and fuel was very low in all analyzed categories. The total consumed electricity and transportation in the three treated systems represented less than 20% of the inputs in all categories, excepting the ODP category for the electricity consumption (47%). Most of analyzed categories, the storage phase for SBi and SABi systems represented about 100% of environmental implications.
Environmental profile per FU and slurry N value of conventional and treated systems analyzed in this study.

In general, the environmental impact was mainly due to the added treatments during storage phase and not to the consumed resources (electricity and transportation) in all steps of process as in the conven- tional system (Fig. 3). Hence, the use of additives during the storage period dominates the environmental impacts, although the emissions and costs associated with its transportation were excluded. However, the acidification without the addition of biochar (SA) showed the highest efficiency in reducing impact in all categories in comparison with the biochar and acid plus biochar combination systems (Table 4). The advantage of the SA system was more noticeable when the environ- mental impact was expressed considering slurry nitrogen value (envi- ronmental impacts per kg of N in the slurry). Taking into account the nitrogen value of the slurry, the GWP values are close to between S and SA systems and the ODP value of the SA system was lower than that of untreated slurry. All treated systems showed the lowest impact towards EP and ODP categories. In addition, a major impact of storage was observed on GWP during the modeled period of 100 years, especially in SBi and SABi systems, 93.4 kg CO2-eq and 91.3 kg CO2-eq, respectively (Table 4, Fig. 3). In total, the additives represented up to 95% of the inputs for the AP, EP, GWP and HTP categories in SBi and SABi systems (Fig. 3). Sulphuric acid in SA system represented 59% of the inputs for ODP category and 71, 81, 92 and 98%, respectively for the EP, GWP, HTP and AP impact categories analyzed in this study.
