34: The results on gas emissions from the laboratory assay were used on the LCA methodology to assess the environmental impact of the treat- ments from slurry collection to soil application. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study reporting results from LCA methodol- ogy on the assessment of the environmental impacts associated to slurry treatment by acidification and biochar.

To understand the true effects of the mitigation actions under GHG emissions, its measuring and monitoring require the description of the changed parameters, the calculated targeted emission type and related side effects (Leip et al., 2017). In this study, the selected five environ- mental impact categories are commonly used in similar studies of LCA (De Vries et al., 2012; Salvador et al., 2016; Manso et al., 2018). The results of this environmental analysis demonstrated that the treated system have a major impact on GWP, AP and HTP categories mainly, due to addition of additives in storage period. The contribution of consumed energy and transportation was very low in all impact categories, agreeing with ten Hoeve et al. (2016).

Towards acidification potential, the overall impact scores are ascending in treated systems with 0.05, 0.21, 0.26 kg of SO2-equivalent for SA, SBi and SABi, respectively (Table 4). The highest contribution has come from the storage process that included the addition of addi- tives on liquid slurry (Fig. 3). For this category, ammonia (NH3-N) emissions are considered as main contributor to environmental impact; however, other characteristic nitrogenous emissions in terms of N2, N2O, NO3, and SO2 have also been emitted during storage period and field application (Rasheed et al., 2019). Salvador et al. (2016) showed that the AP is strongly influenced by slurry storage and the contribution of consumed energy and fuel was very low, what agree with our results.

The freshwater eutrophication potential expresses the excessive nu- trients in water bodies originated, for example, from deposition of ni- trogen, mineral fertilizers and soil erosion (Rasheed et al., 2019). For this impact category, SBi and SABi systems have reflected maximum impacts linked to storage period, similarly to GWP category. However, the LF acidification no affects the EP, as described by ten Hoeve et al. (2016). Rasheed et al. (2019) described the P as a non-gaseous element with indirect emissions to the atmosphere between the storage period and the P leach moment after the application of compost on the soil. Zhang et al. (2013) reported that the NH3 emissions are main

Fig. 3. Impact of treated systems (S, SA, SBi and SABi) in terms of selected categories like acidification potential (AP), freshwater eutrophication potential (EP), global warming potential (GWP), human toxicity potential (HTP) and ozone layer depletion potential (ODP), considering the different unit-processes of slurry management (mechanical separation (MS), storage (St) and field application (FA)) of each system. T: transport, E: energy, A: additive.

The global warming potential is based on CO2, N2O and CH4 emis- sions. Facing the growing of livestock sector, these GHG emissions are increasing, CH4 and N2O mainly in comparison with CO2 on GWP (Bastami et al., 2020). Rasheed et al. (2019) reported that largest climate impact contribution can result of the highest loss of organic matter and most respective emissions in the storage period. In addition, in the organic matter a significant amount of C would eventually be emitted as CO2 when the slurry was applied to the farmlands with consequently climate changes. Although the farm conditions and func- tional unit were different, Salvador et al. (2016) observed that about 59% of the total GHG emissions were resulting from the enteric emis- sions plus slurry storage, already the crop production no significantly influence the GWP, resulting in the emissions of the crops processing and the variety of C stock in the soil. Additionally, although studies have been demonstrated that the separated storage of slurry fractions empower the GWP impact, data of the SF and LF emissions during storage are scarce (Dinuccio et al., 2008; De Vries et al., 2015). Similarly with our study, ten Hoeve et al. (2016) for GWP demonstrated that slurry acidification has a 165% higher impact on this category than non-acidified LF.

The GHG and NH3 emissions from slurry storage phase of this study demonstrated as these emissions contribute strongly to environmental impacts, on GWP and AP categories mainly. These important parameters can affect final results, as the NH3 emission during processing and CH4 emission from slurry storage. In slurry storage, the CH4 emissions have been described as an important parameter that influences the GWP and

GHG balance along of slurry management systems (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009; De Vries et al., 2012). To calculate a substitution rate of adequate mineral fertilizer, De Vries et al. (2012) also highlighted to N loss from slurry storage should be considered prior to management.

For human toxicity potential, SBi and SABi systems have reflected maximum impacts and it were observed during storage period (3.49 and 3.69 kg of DCB-equivalent respectively), similarly to EP and GWP category. Between the treated systems, these demonstrated a marked increase in HTP impact when compared to the SA system that achieved the highest impact reduction (0.22 kg of DCB-equivalent). The human toxicity is based on substance number with a potential human health impact (Zhang et al., 2013). In the SBi and SABi systems, the impact in both comes mainly from storage period where was applied the biochar, common additive in both systems. In recent publication, this additive has been demonstrated a negative potential impact on human health due to risks of dust inhalation as described by Gelardi et al. (2019). Since biochar shows high porosity and low bulk density through of mechanical soil disturbance or via natural can occur release to atmospheric (Gelardi et al., 2019). As described by ten Hoeve et al. (2016), human toxicity potential was not affected by liquid fraction acidification, agreeing with our study since the acidification showed the smallest environmental impact on human toxicity when compared to other treated systems.

Towards ozone layer depletion potential (ODP), the overall impact values are increasing from system SA, SBi to SABi as 3.54 x10 11 , 8.55 x10 11 and 1.06 x10 -10 kg of R11-equivalent, respectively (Table 4). The highest impact has come from the storage period following by mechanical separation.

Considering the slurry nitrogen value in the impact categories analyzed (Table 4), the results confirmed the advantage of acidification on mitigation of environmental impacts and additionally cattle-effluent valorization. The environmental impacts observed by SA system were attenuated, improving the final value of slurry in nitrogen when it was applied to land and increasing yields. However, this system also demonstrated the smallest environmental impacts in comparison with other treated systems. Furthermore, the impact of addition of sulphuric acid in SA system was attenuated when compared to reference system. This is corroborated by Sommer et al. (2015) and Jensen et al. (2020), reporting that the acidification process is responsible for higher emis- sions at GWP, although these are off-set by avoided crop production with higher substitution of fertilizers compared to the conventional system. In pig slurry, Regueiro et al. (2016) observed that a combination of separation by screw-press and acidification improved its properties, mostly nitrogen and the potential N mineralization. An efficient way to improve the slurry-N use with significant gas emission reduction in- cludes adequate slurry acidification, mitigating the hazardous environ- mental impacts without affecting crop production (Park et al., 2018).
35: The results on gas emissions from the laboratory assay were used on the LCA methodology to assess the environmental impact of the treat- ments from slurry collection to soil application. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study reporting results from LCA methodol- ogy on the assessment of the environmental impacts associated to slurry treatment by acidification and biochar.

To understand the true effects of the mitigation actions under GHG emissions, its measuring and monitoring require the description of the changed parameters, the calculated targeted emission type and related side effects (Leip et al., 2017). In this study, the selected five environ- mental impact categories are commonly used in similar studies of LCA (De Vries et al., 2012; Salvador et al., 2016; Manso et al., 2018). The results of this environmental analysis demonstrated that the treated system have a major impact on GWP, AP and HTP categories mainly, due to addition of additives in storage period. The contribution of consumed energy and transportation was very low in all impact categories, agreeing with ten Hoeve et al. (2016).

Towards acidification potential, the overall impact scores are ascending in treated systems with 0.05, 0.21, 0.26 kg of SO2-equivalent for SA, SBi and SABi, respectively (Table 4). The highest contribution has come from the storage process that included the addition of addi- tives on liquid slurry (Fig. 3). For this category, ammonia (NH3-N) emissions are considered as main contributor to environmental impact; however, other characteristic nitrogenous emissions in terms of N2, N2O, NO3, and SO2 have also been emitted during storage period and field application (Rasheed et al., 2019). Salvador et al. (2016) showed that the AP is strongly influenced by slurry storage and the contribution of consumed energy and fuel was very low, what agree with our results.

The freshwater eutrophication potential expresses the excessive nu- trients in water bodies originated, for example, from deposition of ni- trogen, mineral fertilizers and soil erosion (Rasheed et al., 2019). For this impact category, SBi and SABi systems have reflected maximum impacts linked to storage period, similarly to GWP category. However, the LF acidification no affects the EP, as described by ten Hoeve et al. (2016). Rasheed et al. (2019) described the P as a non-gaseous element with indirect emissions to the atmosphere between the storage period and the P leach moment after the application of compost on the soil. Zhang et al. (2013) reported that the NH3 emissions are main

The contribution of consumed energy and transportation was very low in all impact categories, agreeing with ten Hoeve et al. (2016).

Towards acidification potential, the overall impact scores are ascending in treated systems with 0.05, 0.21, 0.26 kg of SO2-equivalent for SA, SBi and SABi, respectively (Table 4). The highest contribution has come from the storage process that included the addition of addi- tives on liquid slurry (Fig. 3). For this category, ammonia (NH3-N) emissions are considered as main contributor to environmental impact; however, other characteristic nitrogenous emissions in terms of N2, N2O, NO3, and SO2 have also been emitted during storage period and field application (Rasheed et al., 2019). Salvador et al. (2016) showed that the AP is strongly influenced by slurry storage and the contribution of consumed energy and fuel was very low, what agree with our results.

The freshwater eutrophication potential expresses the excessive nu- trients in water bodies originated, for example, from deposition of ni- trogen, mineral fertilizers and soil erosion (Rasheed et al., 2019). For this impact category, SBi and SABi systems have reflected maximum impacts linked to storage period, similarly to GWP category. However, the LF acidification no affects the EP, as described by ten Hoeve et al. (2016). Rasheed et al. (2019) described the P as a non-gaseous element with indirect emissions to the atmosphere between the storage period and the P leach moment after the application of compost on the soil. Zhang et al. (2013) reported that the NH3 emissions are main

Fig. 3. Impact of treated systems (S, SA, SBi and SABi) in terms of selected categories like acidification potential (AP), freshwater eutrophication potential (EP), global warming potential (GWP), human toxicity potential (HTP) and ozone layer depletion potential (ODP), considering the different unit-processes of slurry management (mechanical separation (MS), storage (St) and field application (FA)) of each system. T: transport, E: energy, A: additive.

The global warming potential is based on CO2, N2O and CH4 emis- sions. Facing the growing of livestock sector, these GHG emissions are increasing, CH4 and N2O mainly in comparison with CO2 on GWP (Bastami et al., 2020). Rasheed et al. (2019) reported that largest climate impact contribution can result of the highest loss of organic matter and most respective emissions in the storage period. In addition, in the organic matter a significant amount of C would eventually be emitted as CO2 when the slurry was applied to the farmlands with consequently climate changes. Although the farm conditions and func- tional unit were different, Salvador et al. (2016) observed that about 59% of the total GHG emissions were resulting from the enteric emis- sions plus slurry storage, already the crop production no significantly influence the GWP, resulting in the emissions of the crops processing and the variety of C stock in the soil. Additionally, although studies have been demonstrated that the separated storage of slurry fractions empower the GWP impact, data of the SF and LF emissions during storage are scarce (Dinuccio et al., 2008; De Vries et al., 2015). Similarly with our study, ten Hoeve et al. (2016) for GWP demonstrated that slurry acidification has a 165% higher impact on this category than non-acidified LF.

The GHG and NH3 emissions from slurry storage phase of this study demonstrated as these emissions contribute strongly to environmental impacts, on GWP and AP categories mainly. These important parameters can affect final results, as the NH3 emission during processing and CH4 emission from slurry storage. In slurry storage, the CH4 emissions have been described as an important parameter that influences the GWP and

GHG balance along of slurry management systems (Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2009; De Vries et al., 2012). To calculate a substitution rate of adequate mineral fertilizer, De Vries et al. (2012) also highlighted to N loss from slurry storage should be considered prior to management.

For human toxicity potential, SBi and SABi systems have reflected maximum impacts and it were observed during storage period (3.49 and 3.69 kg of DCB-equivalent respectively), similarly to EP and GWP category. Between the treated systems, these demonstrated a marked increase in HTP impact when compared to the SA system that achieved the highest impact reduction (0.22 kg of DCB-equivalent). The human toxicity is based on substance number with a potential human health impact (Zhang et al., 2013). In the SBi and SABi systems, the impact in both comes mainly from storage period where was applied the biochar, common additive in both systems. In recent publication, this additive has been demonstrated a negative potential impact on human health due to risks of dust inhalation as described by Gelardi et al. (2019). Since biochar shows high porosity and low bulk density through of mechanical soil disturbance or via natural can occur release to atmospheric (Gelardi et al., 2019). As described by ten Hoeve et al. (2016), human toxicity potential was not affected by liquid fraction acidification, agreeing with our study since the acidification showed the smallest environmental impact on human toxicity when compared to other treated systems.

Towards ozone layer depletion potential (ODP), the overall impact values are increasing from system SA, SBi to SABi as 3.54 x10 11 , 8.55 x10 11 and 1.06 x10 -10 kg of R11-equivalent, respectively (Table 4). The highest impact has come from the storage period following by mechanical separation.

Considering the slurry nitrogen value in the impact categories analyzed (Table 4), the results confirmed the advantage of acidification on mitigation of environmental impacts and additionally cattle-effluent valorization. The environmental impacts observed by SA system were attenuated, improving the final value of slurry in nitrogen when it was applied to land and increasing yields. However, this system also demonstrated the smallest environmental impacts in comparison with other treated systems. Furthermore, the impact of addition of sulphuric acid in SA system was attenuated when compared to reference system. This is corroborated by Sommer et al. (2015) and Jensen et al. (2020), reporting that the acidification process is responsible for higher emis- sions at GWP, although these are off-set by avoided crop production with higher substitution of fertilizers compared to the conventional system. In pig slurry, Regueiro et al. (2016) observed that a combination of separation by screw-press and acidification improved its properties, mostly nitrogen and the potential N mineralization. An efficient way to improve the slurry-N use with significant gas emission reduction in- cludes adequate slurry acidification, mitigating the hazardous environ- mental impacts without affecting crop production (Park et al., 2018).

43: <header>4.2. GHG and NH3 emissions and added treatments </header>

In many livestock farms a surplus of nutrients is produced in relation to crop requirements, increasing the environmental problems due to the risk of nutrients losses to the environment. A common practice at the farm scale is the separation of slurry into a liquid and a nutrient-rich solid fraction. This can be an effective technique that increases the ef- ficiency of nutrients management, reducing the volume of slurry that will need to be transported to fields close to the farm and the cost of transporting slurry (Burton, 1997; Moller et al., 2000, 2002). The me- chanical and chemical treatment of slurry at the time of fraction sepa- ration allows decreasing slurry dry matter content; in addition, these have the potential to reduce gaseous emissions and several products have been tested with this aim (Pereira et al., 2010; Fangueiro et al., 2015a). In this study, the conventional system, in which the slurry was separated in two fractions by screw press, allowed the characterization of the environmental profile of this system during storage period. These results are corroborated by Fangueiro et al. (2015a) that showed a combination of acidification and separation as an efficient strategy to decrease the NH3 emissions and the GWP compared to solid-liquid separation alone (Moller et al., 2000). Although the slurry mechanical separation by decanter centrifuge or screw press reduced by 12% and 8% the total GHG emissions, respectively (Hou et al., 2017), these re- sults demonstrated the need for new strategies to mitigate the envi- ronmental impact through the reduction of gaseous emissions during slurry management practices. Nevertheless, the LCA of this system could not be compared with treated systems, since the addition of additives will increase the environmental impact due the cost/recourses associ- ated with the additives.

An integrated LCA approach for each treatment together with the results obtained from a laboratorial experiment improved not just gas emission impacts but also the assessment of the environmental profile and the impacts from the diesel and consumption energy during the slurry management from the slurry collection to its field application with the acidification and biochar addition as well as the impacts. The cattle slurry acidification allowed a clear reduction of direct gas emis- sions compared to other treated systems. Results from the present study are in agreement with other reports (Kai et al., 2008; Petersen et al., 2012; Hou et al., 2017), showing a decrease of NH3 and GHG emissions with the implementation of slurry acidification during storage. There- fore, all proposed treated systems have positive environmental impacts and they can be considered alternatives or solutions for mitigation of environmentally harmful gases, showing the highest reduction from acidification of cattle-slurry (SA system) on analyzed environmental impact categories compared to other treatments. In addition, slurry acidification is described as a cheaper approach (Fangueiro et al.,

2015b). Sulphuric acid was ranked one of best additives that can minimize emissions of GHG and NH3 from cattle slurry storage and it was considered to be more cost-efficient (Kavanagh et al., 2019). In opposite, Sommer et al. (2015) reported the early slurry acidification and separation as expensive treatments, attenuated if benefits after it application, for example, save cost on the acquirer of mineral fertilizers and increase yields, are taken into account. Although farmers could maintain more N in the process with acidified slurry, the volatilized NH3 decrease and leading to better yields (Kai et al., 2008; ten Hoeve et al., 2016).

During storage, the total GHG emissions in the SA system demon- strated the highest reduction (Table 3), followed by SABi and SBi sys- tem. The interesting results of SABi system relative to GHG emissions opposed with the obtained results for the impact categories, showing a benefic synergic association between sulphuric acid and biochar for emissions. Previous study (Hou et al., 2017) reported the impacts of different treatments on NH3 emissions in European countries, showing the acidification (5%) a large decrease in Denmark, following by nitrification-denitrification treatment in Belgium (4%) and the inciner- ation in UK and the Netherlands (2-3%). Increased implementation of acidification associated to slurry separation decreased both GHG and NH3 emissions by 18% and 10%, respectively. Other recent study, Bas- tami et al. (2020) confirm that the intolerance by the methanogens in the slurry acidification was probably the key to the mechanism of inhibiting methane production via self-acidification during storage period and the acidification demonstrated has benefits on the reduction of hazardous environmentally gases.

Slurry management alternatives for GHG mitigation can be expanded by flexible strategies and financial incentives. The environmental per- formance of a farm depends on a variety of factors like the quantity and properties of slurry, financing issues, degree of local technologic knowledge and infrastructure considering collection and transport, available land area, geographical area, climate and legislative devel- opment (Bogner et al., 2008; Laca et al., 2019).

This can be an effective technique that increases the ef- ficiency of nutrients management, reducing the volume of slurry that will need to be transported to fields close to the farm and the cost of transporting slurry (Burton, 1997; Moller et al., 2000, 2002). The me- chanical and chemical treatment of slurry at the time of fraction sepa- ration allows decreasing slurry dry matter content; in addition, these have the potential to reduce gaseous emissions and several products have been tested with this aim (Pereira et al., 2010; Fangueiro et al., 2015a). In this study, the conventional system, in which the slurry was separated in two fractions by screw press, allowed the characterization of the environmental profile of this system during storage period. These results are corroborated by Fangueiro et al. (2015a) that showed a combination of acidification and separation as an efficient strategy to decrease the NH3 emissions and the GWP compared to solid-liquid separation alone (Moller et al., 2000). Although the slurry mechanical separation by decanter centrifuge or screw press reduced by 12% and 8% the total GHG emissions, respectively (Hou et al., 2017), these re- sults demonstrated the need for new strategies to mitigate the envi- ronmental impact through the reduction of gaseous emissions during slurry management practices. Nevertheless, the LCA of this system could not be compared with treated systems, since the addition of additives will increase the environmental impact due the cost/recourses associ- ated with the additives.

An integrated LCA approach for each treatment together with the results obtained from a laboratorial experiment improved not just gas emission impacts but also the assessment of the environmental profile and the impacts from the diesel and consumption energy during the slurry management from the slurry collection to its field application with the acidification and biochar addition as well as the impacts. The cattle slurry acidification allowed a clear reduction of direct gas emis- sions compared to other treated systems. Results from the present study are in agreement with other reports (Kai et al., 2008; Petersen et al., 2012; Hou et al., 2017), showing a decrease of NH3 and GHG emissions with the implementation of slurry acidification during storage. There- fore, all proposed treated systems have positive environmental impacts and they can be considered alternatives or solutions for mitigation of environmentally harmful gases, showing the highest reduction from acidification of cattle-slurry (SA system) on analyzed environmental impact categories compared to other treatments. In addition, slurry acidification is described as a cheaper approach (Fangueiro et al.,

2015b). Sulphuric acid was ranked one of best additives that can minimize emissions of GHG and NH3 from cattle slurry storage and it was considered to be more cost-efficient (Kavanagh et al., 2019). In opposite, Sommer et al. (2015) reported the early slurry acidification and separation as expensive treatments, attenuated if benefits after it application, for example, save cost on the acquirer of mineral fertilizers and increase yields, are taken into account. Although farmers could maintain more N in the process with acidified slurry, the volatilized NH3 decrease and leading to better yields (Kai et al., 2008; ten Hoeve et al., 2016).

During storage, the total GHG emissions in the SA system demon- strated the highest reduction (Table 3), followed by SABi and SBi sys- tem. The interesting results of SABi system relative to GHG emissions opposed with the obtained results for the impact categories, showing a benefic synergic association between sulphuric acid and biochar for emissions. Previous study (Hou et al., 2017) reported the impacts of different treatments on NH3 emissions in European countries, showing the acidification (5%) a large decrease in Denmark, following by nitrification-denitrification treatment in Belgium (4%) and the inciner- ation in UK and the Netherlands (2-3%). Increased implementation of acidification associated to slurry separation decreased both GHG and NH3 emissions by 18% and 10%, respectively. Other recent study, Bas- tami et al. (2020) confirm that the intolerance by the methanogens in the slurry acidification was probably the key to the mechanism of inhibiting methane production via self-acidification during storage period and the acidification demonstrated has benefits on the reduction of hazardous environmentally gases.

Slurry management alternatives for GHG mitigation can be expanded by flexible strategies and financial incentives. The environmental per- formance of a farm depends on a variety of factors like the quantity and properties of slurry, financing issues, degree of local technologic knowledge and infrastructure considering collection and transport, available land area, geographical area, climate and legislative devel- opment (Bogner et al., 2008; Laca et al., 2019).

48: Facing environmental pressures, efficient technologies should be affordable and sustainable in the long term to achieve the combined benefits of agricultural infrastructure and reduction of gaseous emis- sions (Bogner et al., 2001). The LCA demonstrate an important tool of decision to identify the environmental consequences around slurry management; however, several limitations were found in this study, being the mainly one the comparability between studies. Mostly of the LCA studies performed in animal production on GHG emissions were in pigs (e.g. ten Hoeve et al., 2016). On the one land, the diversity of treatment techniques used or farm conditions difficult the comparison between our study and performed studies and, the chosen phase of the analyzed process also varies. On the other hand, the chosen unit func- tional varies between similar studies and economic information is scarce in most of consulted studies. The obtained results in our study are partially comparable with those reported by others (Aguirre-Villegas et al., 2014; De Vries et al., 2015; ten Hoeve et al., 2016; Rasheed et al., 2019), regarding identical scenarios and FU of 1 ton of fresh dairy cow slurry. The characterization factors were different, for example 21 for CH4 and 310 for N2O compared with 25 for CH4 and 289 for N2O in this study. Emissions from activities as mechanical separation and field application of slurry were also considered in greater detail in this study and different sources of emission factors were used. In last years, the published studies number about biochar continues to exponentially increase. Although LCA using biochar as additive in slurry during storage is scarce, to the best of our knowledge this is the first trial using biochar for this purpose during storage. Most of studies using biochar reported its production process and use to mitigate GHG on field application period (Steiner et al., 2007; Roberts et al., 2010; Brennan et al., 2015; Munoz et al., 2017), showing differences in used

feedstock and production technologies mainly, in which difficult the comparison between studies and the estimation the effects of them on soil. In this study we not consider the feed and milk production and the emissions that occur in field application were considered equal for all systems due to lack of studies with similar conditions. However, a study reported that the slurry acidification reduced the cumulative NH3 loss by 92% relative to the LF, after field application, increasing their fertilizer value in NH4 and S (Owusu-Twum et al., 2017). While the acidified cattle-slurry in another field experiment resulted in decreasing the NH3 emission by 63% and increasing the grass crop yield from 8 t/ha up to 25 t/ha (Mazur et al., 2019). Supplementation of acidified pig-slurry just before its application on the land reduced NH3, N2O emissions and NO3 leaching, 78.1%, 78.9% and 17.81% respectively (Park et al., 2018). With regard to biochar, their properties and soil characteristics are described as key factors to control of NH3 emissions on field applica- tions. In a study where the soil was amended with pig slurry following the application of biochar to the field, increased NH3 emissions were observed in comparison with the control, due to the high hydropho- bicity, low ability to absorb NH4 of additive and strong slurry pH buff- ering (Subedi et al., 2015). In slurry management process, the solid fraction storage was not included, as well as, the effect of slurry appli- cation as nutrients and mineral fertilizer on farmland.

50: This study expands the knowledge about the environmental impact of cattle slurry treated with three different strategies to reduce gas emissions (acid, biochar and acid + biochar), using as evaluating tools a laboratory experiment complemented by the LCA methodology. The laboratory assay showed that sulphuric acid was the most effi- cient strategy and it is technically applicable. Additionally, the LCA profile highlighted the SA system (with slurry acidification) as an effective technique to reduce other environmental implications from livestock production and to improve cattle-effluent valorization. Therefore, mechanical separation together with sulphuric acid addition reveals less environmental implications from wastewater management of animal origin.
